en.extract:
The southern house wren (Troglodytes musculus) is a very small passerine bird in the wren family Troglodytidae. It is found from southern Mexico to southern Chile and southern Argentina. The name troglodytes means "hole dweller", and is a reference to the bird's tendency to disappear into crevices when hunting insects or to seek shelter. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the northern house wren (Troglodytes aedon).


Taxonomy
The southern house wren was formally described in 1823 by the German naturalist Johann Andreas Naumann under the binomial name Troglodytes musculus. He specified the type locality as the state of Bahia in eastern Brazil. The specific epithet is Latin meaning "little mouse". The southern house wren was formerly considered to be part of the house wren complex that also included the northern house wren and six insular forms. The southern house wren was split from the complex based on the deep genetic divergence, differences in vocalizations and differences in morphology.
Twenty-one subspecies are recognised:

T. m. intermedius Cabanis, 1861 – south Mexico (southeast Veracruz and west Chiapas) to central Costa Rica
T. m. peninsularis Nelson, 1901 – Yucatán Peninsula (southeast Mexico)
T. m. inquietus Baird, SF, 1864 – southwest Costa Rica to east Panama
T. m. carychrous Wetmore, 1957 – Coiba (off south Panama)
T. m. pallidipes Phillips, AR, 1986 – Pearl Islands (off southeast Panama)
T. m. tobagensis Lawrence, 1888 – Tobago (north of Trinidad)
T. m. atopus Oberholser, 1904 – north Colombia
T. m. effutitus Wetmore, 1958 – Guajira Pen. (north Colombia) and northwest Venezuela
T. m. striatulus (Lafresnaye, 1845) – west, central Colombia and northwest Venezuela
T. m. columbae Stone, 1899 – east Colombia and west Venezuela
T. m. clarus Berlepsch & Hartert, EJO, 1902 – Venezuela (except the west), east Colombia and the Guianas to northeast Peru and north, west Brazil
T. m. albicans Berlepsch & Taczanowski, 1884 – southwest Colombia and west Ecuador
T. m. musculus Naumann, JF, 1823 – central, east Brazil to northeast Argentina and east Paraguay
T. m. bonariae Hellmayr, 1919 – extreme southeast Brazil, Uruguay and northeast Argentina
T. m. puna Berlepsch & Stolzmann, 1896 – Peru and northwest Bolivia
T. m. audax Tschudi, 1844 – west Peru
T. m. carabayae Chapman & Griscom, 1924 – central, south Peru
T. m. tecellatus d'Orbigny & Lafresnaye, 1837 – southwest Peru and north Chile
T. m. rex Berlepsch & Leverkühn, 1890 – central Bolivia to north Argentina and west Paraguay
T. m. atacamensis Hellmayr, 1924 – north, central Chile
T. m. chilensis Lesson, RP, 1830 – south Chile and south Argentina


Behaviour
In Argentina, southern house wrens dispersed more frequently between-seasons than within a season, with females dispersing more often than males.  Widowed and single males dispersed more frequently than paired males, whilst within-season divorce increased the breeding success of females but not males.
In the subtropical montane forest of northwestern Argentina and similar habitat, the southern house wren breeds in the rainy summer months from late October to late December. Brood loss due to predation was found to be light in the Southern Andean Yungas, with predation of nestling young being almost insignificant.


References

The '''southern house wren''' ('''''Troglodytes musculus''''') is a very small [[passerine]] bird in the wren [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Troglodytidae]]. It is found from southern Mexico to southern Chile and southern Argentina. The name ''troglodytes'' means "hole dweller", and is a reference to the bird's tendency to disappear into crevices when hunting insects or to seek shelter. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[northern house wren]] (''Troglodytes aedon'').

==Taxonomy==
The southern house wren was [[species description|formally described]] in 1823 by the German naturalist [[Johann Andreas Naumann]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Troglodytes musculus''. He specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as the state of [[Bahia]] in eastern Brazil.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Naumann | first=Johann Andreas | author-link=Johann Andreas Naumann | date=1823 | title=Naturgeschichte der Vögel Deutschlands, nach eigenen Erfahrungen entworfen | volume=3 | language=German | location=Leipzig | publisher=G. Fleischer | at=Table facing p. 724 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33763649 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=426 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481101 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Latin]] meaning "little mouse".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=musculus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=musculus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=8 October 2024 }}</ref> The southern house wren was formerly considered to be part of the house wren complex that also included the [[northern house wren]] and six insular forms. The southern house wren was split from the complex based on the deep [[genetic divergence]],<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Klicka | first1=J. | last2=Epperly | first2=K. | last3=Smith | first3=B.T. | last4=Spellman | first4=G.M. | last5=Chaves | first5=J.A. | last6=Escalante | first6=P. | last7=Witt | first7=C.C. | last8=Canales-del-Castillo | first8=R. | last9=Zink | first9=R.M. | date=2023 | title=Lineage diversity in a widely distributed New World passerine bird, the House Wren | journal=Ornithology | volume=140 | issue=3 | article-number=ukad018 | doi=10.1093/ornithology/ukad018 | doi-access=free }}</ref> differences in vocalizations<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sosa-López | first1=J. Roberto | last2=Mennill | first2=Daniel J. | date=2014 | title=Continent-wide patterns of divergence in acoustic and morphological traits in the House Wren species complex | journal=The Auk | volume=131 | issue=1 | pages=41–54 | doi=10.1642/AUK-13-161.1 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sosa-López | first1=J.R. | last2=Martínez Gómez | first2=J.E. | last3=Mennill | first3=D.J. | date=2016 | title=Divergence in mating signals correlates with genetic distance and behavioural responses to playback | journal=Journal of Evolutionary Biology | volume=29 | issue=2 | pages=306–318 | doi=10.1111/jeb.12782 | doi-access=free | pmid=26528860 }}</ref> and differences in morphology.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Oberholser | first=Harry C. | author-link=Harry C. Oberholser | date=1904 | title=A review of the wrens of the genus ''Troglodytes'' | journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum | volume=27 | issue=1354 | pages=197–210 | doi=10.5479/si.00963801.27-1354.197 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Dapple-throats, sugarbirds, fairy-bluebirds, kinglets, hyliotas, wrens & gnatcatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/sugarbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 October 2024 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Chesser | first1=R.T. | last2=Billerman | first2=S.M. | last3=Burns | first3=K.J. | last4=Cicero | first4=C. | last5=Dunn | first5=J.L. | last6=Hernández-Baños | first6=B.E. | last7=Jiménez | first7=R.A. | last8=Johnson | first8=O. | last9=Kratter | first9=A.W. | last10=Mason | first10=N.A. | last11=Rasmussen | first11=P.C. | last12=Remsen | first12=J.V.J. | date=2024 | title=Sixty-fifth Supplement to the American Ornithological Society's Check-list of North American Birds | journal=Ornithology | volume=141 | issue=3 | article-number=ukae019 | doi=10.1093/ornithology/ukae019 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

Twenty-one [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''T. m. intermedius'' [[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]], 1861 – south Mexico (southeast [[Veracruz]] and west [[Chiapas]]) to central Costa Rica
* ''T. m. peninsularis'' [[Edward William Nelson|Nelson]], 1901 – [[Yucatán Peninsula]] (southeast Mexico)
* ''T. m. inquietus'' [[Spencer Fullerton Baird|Baird, SF]], 1864 – southwest Costa Rica to east Panama
* ''T. m. carychrous'' [[Alexander Wetmore|Wetmore]], 1957 – [[Coiba]] (off south Panama)
* ''T. m. pallidipes'' [[Allan Robert Phillips|Phillips, AR]], 1986 – [[Pearl Islands]] (off southeast Panama)
* ''T. m. tobagensis'' [[George Newbold Lawrence|Lawrence]], 1888 – [[Tobago]] (north of Trinidad)
* ''T. m. atopus'' [[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1904 – north Colombia
* ''T. m. effutitus'' Wetmore, 1958 – Guajira Pen. (north Colombia) and northwest Venezuela
* ''T. m. striatulus'' ([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1845) – west, central Colombia and northwest Venezuela
* ''T. m. columbae'' [[Witmer Stone|Stone]], 1899 – east Colombia and west Venezuela
* ''T. m. clarus'' [[Hans von Berlepsch|Berlepsch]] & [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1902 – Venezuela (except the west), east Colombia and the Guianas to northeast Peru and north, west Brazil
* ''T. m. albicans'' Berlepsch & [[Władysław Taczanowski|Taczanowski]], 1884 – southwest Colombia and west Ecuador
* ''T. m. musculus'' [[Johann Friedrich Naumann|Naumann, JF]], 1823 – central, east Brazil to northeast Argentina and east Paraguay
* ''T. m. bonariae'' [[Carl Eduard Hellmayr|Hellmayr]], 1919 – extreme southeast Brazil, Uruguay and northeast Argentina
* ''T. m. puna'' Berlepsch & [[Jan Sztolcman|Stolzmann]], 1896 – Peru and northwest Bolivia
* ''T. m. audax'' [[Johann Jakob von Tschudi|Tschudi]], 1844 – west Peru
* ''T. m. carabayae'' [[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]] & [[Ludlow Griscom|Griscom]], 1924 – central, south Peru
* ''T. m. tecellatus'' [[Alcide d'Orbigny|d'Orbigny]] & Lafresnaye, 1837 – southwest Peru and north Chile
* ''T. m. rex'' Berlepsch & [[Paul Leverkühn|Leverkühn]], 1890 – central Bolivia to north Argentina and west Paraguay
* ''T. m. atacamensis'' Hellmayr, 1924 – north, central Chile
* ''T. m. chilensis'' [[René Lesson|Lesson, RP]], 1830 – south Chile and south Argentina

==Behaviour==
In Argentina, southern house wrens dispersed more frequently between-seasons than within a season, with females dispersing more often than males.  Widowed and single males dispersed more frequently than paired males, whilst within-season divorce increased the breeding success of females but not males.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Carro, M. E. |author2=Llambías, P. E. |author3=Fernández, G. J.  | year=2017| title=Mate and territory availability affect breeding dispersal and divorce in a resident Southern House Wren ''Troglodytes aedon musculus'' population| journal=Ibis| volume=159| pages=168–179| doi=10.1111/ibi.12438| issue=1| hdl=11336/45240| hdl-access=free}}</ref>

In the [[subtropical]] [[montane forest]] of northwestern Argentina and similar habitat, the southern house wren breeds in the rainy summer months from late October to late December. Brood loss due to [[predator|predation]] was found to be light in the Southern Andean [[Yungas]], with predation of nestling young being almost insignificant.<ref name = aueretal2007>{{cite journal|author1=Auer, Sonya K. |author2=Bassar, Ronald D. |author3=Fontaine, Joseph J. |author4=Martin, Thomas E. |year=2007|title= Breeding biology of passerines in a subtropical montane forest in Northwestern Argentina|journal=[[Condor (journal)|Condor]]|volume=109|issue=2|pages= 321–333|doi=10.1650/0010-5422(2007)109[321:BBOPIA]2.0.CO;2|s2cid=3675989 |url= https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1001&context=ncfwrustaff |doi-access=free}}</ref>
